chapters.title: The Rise of Nationalism in Europe
topics: French Revolution and the idea of the Nation
The Making of Germany and Italy
Unification of Italy under Mazzini, Garibaldi, Cavour
dates: 1815 — Congress of Vienna — Treaty of Vienna
timeline: 1789: French Revolution → Idea of nationalism born
1815: Congress of Vienna → Restoration of old order
1848: Frankfurt Parliament — attempt at German unification fails
1871: Germany unified under Kaiser William I (Bismarck's policy of Blood and Iron)
importantQuestions: How did the French Revolution create a sense of collective identity?
Explain the role of Bismarck in the unification of Germany.
Describe the process of Italian unification.
What was the role of culture and language in creating national feelings in Europe?
What was the Congress of Vienna? What were its decisions?
tips: Germany: Bismarck (Blood & Iron policy, 3 wars) | Italy: Mazzini (Young Italy), Garibaldi (volunteers), Cavour (diplomacy)
Allegory figures: Marianne = France, Germania = Germany
Dates yaad karne ke liye timeline chart banao aur daily revise karo
Napoleon Civil Code = Equality before law, abolition of feudalism — iske effects important hain

chapters.title: Nationalism in India
topics: First World War, Khilafat, and Non-Cooperation
Sense of collective belonging — Symbols, Icons, Songs
dates: 1919 — Rowlatt Act; Jallianwala Bagh Massacre (13 April)
1922 — Chauri Chaura incident; NCM called off
1930 — Salt March / Dandi March (12 March); CDM begins
importantQuestions: Why was the Non-Cooperation Movement launched? Why was it called off?
Describe the Salt March and its significance.
How did different social groups participate in the Civil Disobedience Movement?
What was the Rowlatt Act? Why did Indians oppose it?
Explain the idea of Satyagraha as understood by Gandhiji.
tips: NCM (1920-22) aur CDM (1930-34) ke differences clearly yaad karo — methods alag the
Chauri Chaura = violence → Gandhi ne NCM wapas liya — cause and effect yaad rakho
Salt March: Sabarmati → Dandi, 240 miles, 24 days — yeh facts important hain
Different groups ka participation: Peasants, Workers, Tribals, Women — sab ka role alag tha

chapters.title: The Making of a Global World
topics: The Nineteenth Century (1815-1914) — Trade, Migration, Indentured Labour
Post-War settlements — Bretton Woods, IMF, World Bank
dates: 1885 — Big European powers met in Berlin to carve up Africa
1944 — Bretton Woods Conference → IMF and World Bank formed
importantQuestions: What was the Great Depression? What were its causes and effects?
What were Silk Routes? How did they link the world?
What was Bretton Woods Agreement? What institutions were created?
What was indentured labour? Why was it compared with slavery?
How has globalisation affected the world in the modern era?
tips: Great Depression: 1929, USA → spread worldwide; Causes: Overproduction, Less consumption, Stock market crash
Bretton Woods → IMF (monetary stability) + World Bank (reconstruction/development)
Silk Routes: Trade + Culture + Religion sab exchange hua — not just silk
Indentured labour = girmitiya — Indians to Caribbean, Fiji, etc.

chapters.title: The Age of Industrialization
topics: Factories come up — first in England
The peculiarities of Industrial growth in India
Market for goods — advertising and labels
importantQuestions: Why was the cotton textile industry struggling in India during colonial rule?
What was proto-industrialisation?
How did Indian textiles decline under British rule?
What role did advertisements play in creating a market for goods?
Who were jobbers? What role did they play in early factories?
tips: Proto-industrialisation = before factory era, production in homes for international market
Indian textiles ka decline = British ne raw cotton export karaya aur finished goods India mein bheche
Jobber = middleman who recruited workers in early factories
Advertisements mein Indian labels — Swadeshi spirit dikhate the

chapters.title: Resources and Development
topics: Types of resources: Natural, Human-made, Human
Development of resources — Planning in India
Soil as a resource — Types of soil in India
importantQuestions: What is resource planning? Why is it essential for India?
Name the different types of soil found in India. Describe any two.
What are the methods of soil conservation?
Explain the classification of resources on different bases.
What is land degradation? What are the ways to solve this problem?
tips: Soil types: Alluvial (most fertile, Indo-Gangetic), Black (Deccan, cotton), Red (less fertile, iron), Laterite (heavy rain areas)
Soil conservation methods: Contour ploughing, Terrace farming, Shelter belts, Strip cropping
Resources ke 3 stages: Identification → Planning → Implementation
Sustainable development ka concept important hai — future generations ke liye bhi resources bachao

chapters.title: Forest and Wildlife Resources
topics: Causes of depletion: Deforestation, Habitat destruction, Hunting, Pollution
Conservation efforts: National Parks, Wildlife Sanctuaries, Biosphere Reserves
Community and Conservation — Chipko Movement, Joint Forest Management
importantQuestions: Why has biodiversity decreased rapidly? Suggest measures to conserve it.
What is the difference between a National Park, Wildlife Sanctuary, and Biosphere Reserve?
Describe the role of communities in forest and wildlife conservation.
What was the Chipko Movement? What was its significance?
How has Wildlife Protection Act 1972 helped conservation?
tips: National Park = strictest protection, no human activity | Wildlife Sanctuary = some activities allowed | Biosphere Reserve = conservation + development both
Chipko Movement 1970s = Sunderlal Bahuguna + villagers hugging trees to prevent deforestation
Species categories: Normal → Vulnerable → Endangered → Extinct — sequence yaad karo

chapters.title: Water Resources
topics: Water scarcity and need for conservation
importantQuestions: Why are multi-purpose river valley projects called the 'temples of modern India'?
What are the advantages and disadvantages of multi-purpose projects?
Describe different traditional methods of rainwater harvesting in India.
Why is water scarcity increasing in India? Suggest solutions.
What is rainwater harvesting? How is it practiced in Rajasthan?
tips: Dams: Bhakra Nangal (Sutlej), Hirakud (Mahanadi), Nagarjuna Sagar (Krishna), Tehri (Bhagirathi)
Criticism of dams: displacement, deforestation, earthquake risk, unequal water distribution
Traditional methods: Rajasthan = Tankas, Khadins; Meghalaya = Bamboo drip irrigation; Tamil Nadu = Eris (tanks)
Rainwater harvesting reduces groundwater depletion — yeh modern importance hai

chapters.title: Agriculture
topics: Major crops: Rice, Wheat, Millets, Maize, Sugarcane, Tea, Coffee, Cotton, Jute, Rubber
importantQuestions: Distinguish between Rabi and Kharif crops with examples.
Why is agriculture important for India's economy?
What are the conditions required for growing rice and wheat?
What were the impacts of Green Revolution?
Explain the different types of farming practiced in India.
tips: Rabi: Oct-March (Wheat, Mustard, Peas) | Kharif: June-Sept (Rice, Maize, Cotton, Jute) | Zaid: March-June (Watermelon, Cucumber)
Rice = high rainfall/irrigation, alluvial soil | Wheat = cool climate, well-drained loamy soil
Green Revolution = 1960s, seeds + fertilizers, Punjab-Haryana | White Revolution = Dairy, V. Kurien
Tea = Assam, Darjeeling, Nilgiris | Coffee = Karnataka (Coorg)

chapters.title: Minerals and Energy Resources
topics: Iron ore, Manganese, Copper, Bauxite, Mica, Limestone
Conventional energy sources: Coal, Petroleum, Natural Gas, Hydel
Non-conventional energy: Solar, Wind, Biogas, Tidal, Geothermal, Nuclear
importantQuestions: Why do we need to conserve mineral resources? Suggest methods.
Describe the distribution of iron ore in India.
Distinguish between conventional and non-conventional sources of energy.
What is the importance of energy resources in economic development?
Why are non-conventional energy sources important for India?
tips: Iron ore belts: Odisha-Jharkhand, Durg-Bastar-Chandrapur, Bellary-Chitradurga-Tumkur, Goa
Coal: Raniganj, Jharia, Bokaro (Jharkhand-Bengal) | Petroleum: Mumbai High (offshore), Digboi (Assam)
Mica: Jharkhand, Bihar, Rajasthan — India = world leader
Non-conventional important kyunki: Renewable, Less pollution, Long-term sustainable

chapters.title: Manufacturing Industries
importantQuestions: Why is the manufacturing sector important for India's development?
Describe the distribution of cotton textile industry in India.
What are the factors affecting the location of an industry?
How does industrial pollution degrade the environment? Suggest measures.
Why is the iron and steel industry called the backbone of modern industry?
tips: Cotton textile: Mumbai (Cottonopolis of India), Ahmedabad (Manchester of India)
Iron & Steel: TISCO (Jamshedpur), SAIL plants (Bhilai, Durgapur, Rourkela, Bokaro)
Sugar industry — Maharashtra (#1), UP, Karnataka — close to sugarcane growing areas
Location factors: Raw material, Market, Transport, Labour, Power, Water, Government policies

chapters.title: Lifelines of National Economy
topics: Transport: Roadways, Railways, Pipelines, Waterways, Airways
importantQuestions: Why are the roadways more important than railways in India?
What is the Golden Quadrilateral? Name the cities it connects.
Why are means of transport and communication called the lifelines of a nation?
Describe the major sea ports of India.
What is the importance of tourism in India's economy?
tips: Golden Quadrilateral: Delhi-Mumbai-Chennai-Kolkata (and back to Delhi) = Super Expressway
National Waterways: NW1 = Ganga (Allahabad-Haldia), NW2 = Brahmaputra (Sadiya-Dhubri), NW3 = Kerala backwaters
Railways = most important for freight and passenger transport in India
Major Ports: Mumbai, Kandla, Kochi, Chennai, Vishakhapatnam, Kolkata-Haldia, Paradwip, Tuticorin, Mangalore

chapters.title: Power Sharing
topics: Belgium and Sri Lanka — case studies
Forms of power sharing: Horizontal, Vertical, Among social groups, Among political parties
importantQuestions: Compare the power sharing arrangements in Belgium and Sri Lanka.
What are the different forms of power sharing in modern democracies?
Why is power sharing desirable? Give two reasons.
What was the Majoritarian policy followed in Sri Lanka?
Describe the community government system in Belgium.
tips: Belgium: 59% Dutch, 40% French, 1% German — phir bhi accommodation (power sharing)
Sri Lanka: Sinhala majority → Majoritarianism → Civil war
Power sharing = prudential (practical — avoids conflict) + moral (right thing to do)
4 forms: Horizontal (legislature, executive, judiciary), Vertical (central, state, local), Social groups, Political parties/alliances

chapters.title: Federalism
topics: Union List, State List, Concurrent List
How is Federalism practiced in India?
Decentralisation in India — Panchayati Raj, Municipalities
importantQuestions: What are the key features of federalism?
Explain the three-fold distribution of legislative powers (Union, State, Concurrent List).
How has the creation of linguistic states strengthened federalism in India?
Describe the three-tier structure of Panchayati Raj.
What is decentralisation? Why is it important in a democracy?
tips: Union List: Defence, Banking, Foreign affairs (97) | State List: Police, Agriculture, Health (66) | Concurrent: Education, Forest, Marriage (47)
Panchayati Raj: Gram Panchayat → Panchayat Samiti (Block) → Zila Parishad (District)
1/3 seats reserved for women in local bodies (73rd Amendment 1992)
Coalition government = multiple parties form government together — important for federalism

chapters.title: Democracy and Diversity
topics: Case studies: Mexico Olympics, Northern Ireland, Yugoslavia
importantQuestions: What are overlapping and cross-cutting social differences? Explain with examples.
How do social divisions affect politics? Explain.
Describe the 1968 Mexico Olympics incident involving African-American athletes.
What is the difference between a homogeneous and heterogeneous society?
tips: Overlapping differences = jab ek hi group mein saari disadvantages ho (e.g., Dalits in India — poor + low caste)
Cross-cutting = different social categories overlap (e.g., Netherlands — Catholics are both rich and poor)
Mexico Olympics 1968: Tommie Smith and John Carlos raised black-gloved fists — Civil Rights protest
Social division + politics can be positive OR negative — depends on how leaders handle it

chapters.title: Gender, Religion and Caste
topics: Caste in politics vs Politics in caste
importantQuestions: How is communalism a threat to Indian democracy?
Explain the role of caste in Indian politics.
Why is women's political representation low in India? What steps have been taken?
What is secularism? How does the Indian Constitution ensure it?
Differentiate between 'caste in politics' and 'politics in caste'.
tips: Women's representation: Lok Sabha mein only ~14% — much lower than world average
Communalism = using religion for political mobilization → very dangerous for democracy
Indian secularism: No state religion, Freedom to practice any religion, No discrimination based on religion
Caste in politics = caste identities used in elections; Politics in caste = political consciousness in caste groups

chapters.title: Outcomes of Democracy
topics: How do we assess democracy's outcomes?
Economic growth and development in democracy
importantQuestions: Is democracy better than dictatorship? Give arguments.
What outcomes can we expect from democracy?
Does democracy lead to economic growth? Explain.
How does democracy accommodate social diversity?
What is the most basic outcome of democracy?
tips: Democracy may not be fastest in economic growth BUT it ensures dignity, equality, and freedom
Most basic outcome = Accountable + Transparent + Legitimate government
Democracy handles conflicts peacefully — dictatorships suppress them
Social diversity accommodation = majority rule + minority rights — both important

chapters.title: Development
topics: Income and other criteria for development
National income and Per Capita Income
importantQuestions: Why is per capita income not a sufficient criterion for measuring development?
What is HDI? What criteria does it use?
Give examples to show that development goals of different people can be conflicting.
Why is sustainable development important?
Compare development of India, Sri Lanka, and Pakistan using HDI criteria.
tips: HDI criteria: Per Capita Income + Education (Literacy) + Health (Life expectancy) — teeno yaad rakho
Per Capita Income = Total National Income / Total Population
India = Developing country, Sri Lanka has better HDI than India (despite lower income)
Sustainability = meeting present needs without compromising future generations

chapters.title: Sectors of the Indian Economy
importantQuestions: Distinguish between Primary, Secondary, and Tertiary sectors with examples.
Why is the tertiary sector becoming important in India?
What is the difference between organised and unorganised sectors?
What is MGNREGA? What is its significance?
Why does Indian economy show disguised unemployment in agriculture?
tips: Primary: Agriculture, Mining | Secondary: Manufacturing, Construction | Tertiary: Services, Banking, IT
In India: Most employed in Primary, but maximum GDP from Tertiary — yeh contradiction important
MGNREGA: 100 days guaranteed work, ₹200/day min wage, rural areas
Disguised unemployment = zyaada log kaam pe lage hain but output nahi badhta (agriculture mein common)

chapters.title: Money and Credit
topics: Money as a medium of exchange
Modern forms of money — Currency, Deposits
Terms of credit — Interest rate, Collateral, Documentation
importantQuestions: Why is money called a 'medium of exchange'?
What is collateral? Why is it required for a loan?
Explain the difference between formal and informal sources of credit.
What are Self-Help Groups? How do they help the poor?
Why do we need to expand formal sources of credit in India?
tips: Formal sources: Banks, Cooperatives (regulated by RBI) | Informal: Moneylenders, Traders, Friends (NOT regulated)
Collateral = guarantee/security for loan (land, property, insurance, gold etc.)
SHGs: 15-20 women group → save money → give internal loans → lower interest than moneylenders
RBI supervises formal sector only — informal sector mein regulation nahi hai, exploitation hoti hai

chapters.title: Globalisation and the Indian Economy
topics: Factors enabling globalisation: Technology, Trade liberalisation
Fair globalisation — creating opportunities for all
importantQuestions: What is globalisation? What are the factors that have enabled it?
How has globalisation affected Indian economy? (Positive and Negative)
What are MNCs? How do they spread their production?
What is the role of WTO in globalisation?
What is the impact of globalisation on small producers in India?
tips: MNCs invest in other countries for: Cheap labour, Proximity to market, Lower costs, Tax benefits
Globalisation = integration of markets of different countries (trade + investment + technology)
1991: India opened its economy (LPG reforms — Liberalisation, Privatisation, Globalisation)
WTO ke rules mostly developed countries ke favour mein — developing countries ka disadvantage
SEZ = Special Economic Zones — tax benefits for MNCs to set up factories

chapters.title: Consumer Rights
topics: Consumer rights: Right to safety, Right to information, Right to choose, Right to seek redressal, Right to represent
importantQuestions: What are the various rights of consumers under Consumer Protection Act?
Explain the three-tier quasi-judicial system for consumer redressal.
Why is RTI Act 2005 important for consumers?
What are ISI, Agmark, and Hallmark? Where are they used?
How can a consumer file a complaint? What details are required?
tips: Consumer Rights: Safety, Information, Choice, Redressal, Representation — SICRR se yaad karo
Courts: District (< ₹1 crore), State (₹1-10 crore), National (> ₹10 crore)
ISI = industrial products, Agmark = agricultural products, Hallmark = gold jewellery
RTI 2005: Any citizen can ask for information from government offices (₹10 fee)
24 December = National Consumer Day (COPRA enacted on this day in 1986)
